'was an action brought by the respondent bank on a ce bond for £20,000, dated 13th December, 1888, which maranteeing an overdraft, which the respondent bank proposed sgaut in favourof the Burwood Land, Building and Investment ny, Limited, The guarantors were directors of the comand their liability under the bond was limited to £10,000 and the company "without the consent or knowledge of the defendants and the other sureties," for good and valuable to sue it for a certain time then agreed upon, being a time than the period of credit which the bank ought to given the company for payment of the debt; that the bank pursuance of the agreement did give time, and forbear to sue diving the time agreed upon. text: Ultimately the whole £10,000 was paid by them, 'The action was to recover interest upon so much of the £10,000 as remained unpaid after April, 1894. At the trial before Owen J. the appellant and his co-defendants contended that by the agreement of 15th January, 1892, the bank had given time to the company until the 30th June following, and that, this having been done without the assent and knowledge of all the sureties, the whole of the sureties, including the appellant, were exonerated. His Honor ruled that the construction ' of the agreement was one of law for himself, not one of fact for the jury, and held that it did not amount to a giving of time.
citation: Deane v City Bank of Sydney [1904] HCA 44

text: The jury found a verdict for the plaintiff for £3,023 18s. 8d. The appellant and the other defendants moved to make absolute arule nisi for a new trial on several grounds, of which those material to this appeal were, that His Honor was in error in refusing to leave to the jury asa question of fact what agreement 'was made between the bank and the Burwood Land and Investment Company on or about the 15th January, 1892, with reference to the company's overdraft, and the granting of time for the payment thereof, and that His Honor was in error in deciding as a question of law that the agreement made between the bank and the com pany at the date mentioned, with reference to the company's ovel~ draft, was not an agreement binding the bank to give time to the company for the payment of the overdraft. The Full Court (consisting of Dariey C.J., Simpson J. and Pring J.), discharged the rule with costs.

text: Assent does not prevent a surety from taking advantage of an alteration in the instrument of suretyship: Australian Joint Stock Bank v. Bailey (2). 'The rule is that when one surety is released, all are released: Ellesmere Brewery Co. v. Cooper (3). 'The appellant knew that his co-sureties would be released unless their consent was obtained, and therefore the Court must infer that he only assented on the understanding that their consent would be obtained.

text: Cullen (Gordon K.C. with him), for the respondent. 'was no question for the jury to try. All parties acted upon the letter, as containing the substance of the agreement, The appellant in his evidence said: "In pursuance of that letter we gave mortgages &e." The evidence of the conversations was only tendered in order to allow the jury to pronounce upon the legal effect of the document. [O'Connor J.—Your argument is on the assumption that the letter is the econtract.] 'The words of the letter and the other evidence show that the whole agreement was contained in the letter. There was no dis pute as to the words used at the conversation, and the letter merely states the substance of it. If the letter is capable of only one construction, the jury will not be allowed to construe it, simply because the same arrangement was also the subject of a conversation. The only question is the legal effect of admitted words.

text: The Supreme Court has put the only possible construe tion upon the words of the letter, and, as no reasonable jury could find that the coversation in any way varied the effect of it, this Court will not interfere. The cases cited for the appellant are not applicable, because they are cases where there were disputes as to what was actually said, or where the written documents were in conflict with the conversations that were sworn to. [Grirriri C.J.—But it is a question of fact whether the agree ment is contained in the letter only, or in both the letter and the conversation. The letter here purports to be a record of an actual conversation, and refers to it in terms. If there is a verbal offer and a written acceptance, the whole contract must go to the Jury.) text: This is an appeal from a judgment of th Supreme Court of New South Wales refusing to make absolute, rule nisi for a new trial in an action brought by the City Bun of Sydney against Deane and others, sureties under a cash bond given to secure an advance to the Burwood Land, Buil and Investment Co., the principal debtors. 'The question befor us arises on an equitable plea in the following words Honor read the plea as set out above.] The evidence offered prove the agreement consisted of a conversation between th manager of the plaintiff bank and the appellant Deane (who one of the directors of the company, and its solicitor), followed by a letter written to him the next day by the manager of the b together with other cireumstances. At the trial before Mr. i Owen, His Honor ruled that the alleged agreement was conta in the letter, and refused to leave any question to the jury asto the agreement, holding that it was a matter of law for himto determine. It was contended before the Supreme Court for the contained partly in the conversation and partly in the letter, it construction was a question of fact for the jury, and a new tri was asked for on that ground. The learned Judges seem to have differed in opinion somewhat on this point, Darley C.J., and Sin son J., being of opinion that the agreement was contained in th letter only, while Pring J. is reported to have said that the whd evidence of the agreement was to be found in the conversatia and that the letter was immaterial, but that, the terms of th conversation being exactly stated, and uncontradicted, the learn (01) 16N.S.W.

OF AUSTRALIA. ho presi vie FiThe agreement here having been proved solely by J evidence, which was not contradicted, I am of opinion that Fie Honor was right in deciding its meaning asa question of law, Jin refusing to leave it to the jury." Yow it is well settled law that, when an agreement is made evidence, its construction is a matter for the jury, that if, on the other hand, the agreement is in writing, then tis for the Court, not the jury, to construe it. I cannot help ng that, although His Honor is reported to have said this, Je really meant was that, if upon the evidence as toa verbal ment there was nothing to warrant the jury in placing any one construction upon it, there would be no real question for jury, and the Judge would be practically bound to treat the tion as a matter of law, and in that sense what he said nsquite right.

J Deas Tae Cry 'BAXK OF SYDNEY. 1f on the other hand, the plea is read distributively that HIGH COURT extension was given to the company without the consent of sureties severally, there was no evidence of it, because appellant has proved that the extension was granted at his request. Therefore, whichever way the plea is construed, appellant fails, because upon one construction it is a bad and the respondent would be entitled to judgment non veredicto, even if the appellant obtained a verdict; and, upon other, it is not supported by the evidence, because the appell own evidence negatives it.

text: We are told, however, that this is rather hard on the appell because the plea was pleaded in reliance upon the case of tl Australian Joint Stock Bank v. Bailey (1). That was a caseis which an instrument of suretyship was altered after exeeuti with the consent of some but not all of the sureties, and Supreme Court held that the bond was thereby rendered against them all.
citation: Deane v City Bank of Sydney [1904] HCA 44

text: That is an entirely different proposition from the one now set up, viz., that one surety, who requests that time he given to the principal debtor, may take advantage of it as dis. charging himself. That ease, therefore, has no bearing upon present case. Then it was suggested that, if the matter had been left to the jury, or if there were a new trial, it might be possible for the appellant to convince the jury that there wasa term to be implied from the conversation (which was given in detail) that it was intended that the original bond should remain in full force as against all the sureties.
citation: Deane v City Bank of Sydney [1904] HCA 44

text: If it was part of the arrangement that the original bond was so to remain in fore, then the rights would be reserved against the sureties, and there would be nothing in the defence, because the sureties were not discharged. If, on the other hand, that was not a term of the agreement, then, whatever the arrangement was, it was made at the request of the defendant, and he cannot take advantage of it as discharging him. So that, from whatever point of view it is regarded, the position of the defendant is hopeless.
